name: pinning
author: dojo-community
context: Control candidate selection with apt preferences, target releases, apt-mark hold, and repository priority rules
info: This guide explains how APT chooses a candidate version and how to override that decision. It covers target releases, default priority levels, `Pin-Priority` records under `/etc/apt/preferences.d/`, repository-wide preference reduction for third-party archives, and package-level state such as `apt-mark hold`, `auto`, and `manual`. Agents use this node when a package keeps resolving to the wrong source, when a PPA or vendor repository must stay lower priority, or when upgrades should be frozen selectively without disabling the whole repository.
triggers: pin a package version with apt
lower the priority of a third party repository
why is apt choosing this source

sections.title: Default priority levels
sections.body: APT assigns implicit priorities before it evaluates your custom preferences. The especially useful values are 1, 100, 500, and 990, with anything above 1000 required to permit a downgrade. Understanding these defaults is the key to predicting how a custom pin will interact with installed state and target releases.

sections.title: Repository-wide pinning
sections.body: A file under `/etc/apt/preferences.d/` can reduce the priority of an entire external archive. Ubuntu's repository guidance uses `Pin-Priority: 100` as a practical default for a third-party repository that should remain available but not silently replace official packages.

sections.title: Holds, manual installs, and autoremove
sections.body: `apt-mark hold` freezes a package against install, upgrade, and removal. `apt-mark manual` prevents `autoremove` from pruning a package, while `apt-mark auto` makes it eligible for removal once nothing manually installed depends on it. These are install-state controls, not source-preference controls.

sections.title: Validate with apt-cache policy
sections.body: `apt-cache policy` is the verification step for every pinning change. It shows source priorities, candidate selection, and whether a phased update is pinning a version to priority 1 on the current system.

links.context: Use apt-cache policy to inspect the actual outcome of pinning rules
Pair third-party repository setup with explicit preference reduction
Held-back packages can be caused by pinning or phased updates
Use target releases or exact versions for one-off overrides

related.note: Repository management is often incomplete without pinning when third-party sources are involved
Policy inspection is the best way to confirm preference changes

body:
# Pinning, Holds, and Candidate Selection

APT chooses a package candidate by combining installed state, source priorities, target release settings, phased-update rules, and explicit preferences. This node explains the knobs agents can use before forcing package installs ad hoc.

## Default priority model

Debian documents the following important defaults when a target release is set:

- priority `1` for not-automatic archives and versions not phased on this system
- priority `100` for the installed version
- priority `500` for versions outside the target release
- priority `990` for versions in the target release

APT will not downgrade unless the chosen version has a priority above `1000`.

## Repository-wide pinning

```text
# /etc/apt/preferences.d/vendor
Package: *
Pin: origin packages.vendor.example
Pin-Priority: 100
```

Ubuntu's third-party repository guidance recommends lowering precedence for external repositories so official packages are not overwritten by accident. A priority of `100` still allows installs and upgrades of already installed packages from that source. A priority of `1` allows explicit installs but prevents automatic upgrades from that source.

## Target releases

```bash
sudo apt-get install -t noble-backports neovim
```

`-t release` sets a target release for the command and temporarily raises that release's priority. Use it when you want a one-off preference rather than a persistent pin.

## Holds and install-state markers

```bash
sudo apt-mark hold docker-ce
sudo apt-mark unhold docker-ce
apt-mark showhold

sudo apt-mark manual jq
sudo apt-mark auto libjq1
```

`hold` prevents automatic install, upgrade, or removal. `manual` and `auto` control whether a package is eligible for `autoremove`.

## Inspect the result

```bash
apt-cache policy docker-ce
apt-cache policy
```

Always validate pinning with policy output before and after changes. That is the ground truth for the final candidate calculation.
